pages.raw_text:
In resolutions that bore the ear
marks of baving been prepared in
the Spokesman-Review Office a
meeting at Elks' temple last night
demanded concrete bridges at
Monroe street and Olive avenue,
capped City Engineer Ralston for
not pushing along the plans for
concrete bridges at these river
crossings and requested that the
plans for these bridges proceed i'm
nediately; recommended the en-
ployment of Professor VIM. H.
Burr for exporting the plans to be
prepared by the engineer, in the
interest of economical revision"
of the same, If necessary; urged
vigor and dispatch by the city
council and board of public works
in carrying out these orders, and
concluded by ordering a copy Of
the resolutions served on the vari-
GUS heads of the city government


Only small crowd, which
seemed lost in the large room, was
present and many of these left
before the resolutions were pre-.
snted. N. w. Durham, editor of
tn morning paper, officiated as con-
sulting engineer of the evening.


To those In the audience sincere
ly in favor of concrete bridges the
proceedings sounded we'll enough,
where 1t was taken seriously. In
view of the fact that majority of
the city council have expressed
themselves favorable to concrete
bridges built at not excessive
cost over steel, many wondered
why the occasion for the reprint
fuss and clamor.


The vital question with the city
council, as to how the city Is to
secure satisfactory settlement
with the street railway companies
on the distribution of the bridge
cost before proceeding at all,
was not discussed.


The most effective speech from
the standpoint Of argument was
made by Thomas Maloney of the
federal union, who showed the per-
centage of bridge cost that would
remain at home in the case of
concrete or steel. He estimated
that In concrete bridges Is to 80
per cent. of the cost would re-
main at home, while in the case
of steel he placed the home return
at less than 20 per cent.


]. T. O'Brien, member of the
board of public works, came to the
